text:
All WashU first-year students live on campus. As a first-year, you’ll get to live in one of 10 residential communities on a part of campus we call the “South 40.”

Nicknamed “The 40” because it spans forty acres directly south of the Danforth Campus, the South 40 functions like a small town and is home to approximately 3,200 first-year and second-year residents across 24 modern and traditional residence halls. The area features a wide range of amenities, including a fitness center, dining facilities, meeting rooms, basketball and sand volleyball courts, recreation and game rooms, 
music practice rooms
, and campus eateries. Residents also have convenient access to 
Bear Necessities (gift shop)
, 
Paws & Go (convenience store)
, the 
Hitzeman Mail Center
, 
printers and copiers
, and several 
student-run businesses
, making the South 40 a place to study, sleep, eat, relax, and build lasting friendships. First Year Residence Halls

Danforth House
Modern

Dardick House
Modern

Dauten Hall
Traditional

Hitzeman Hall
Traditional

Hurd Hall
Traditional

Koenig House
Modern

Lee Hall
Traditional

Lien House
Modern

Myers Hall
Traditional

Park House
Modern

Rutledge Hall
Traditional

Shanedling Hall
Traditional

Thomas Eliot “A” House
Modern

Umrath House
Modern

Modern suite style buildings

These facilities consist primarily of double rooms, with some single and triple rooms. Most room layouts consist of two double rooms adjoined by a restroom. Modern buildings have elevators. Modern Double

Park House Modern Suite

Traditional buildings (communal and suite style)

These facilities have single and double room options on each floor, and each floor community shares common restrooms. Traditional buildings do not have elevators. Some traditional halls are configured in a suite style with double and single rooms and a shared restroom. Traditional Suite (Dauten, Hitzeman, Hurd, Myers, Rutledge, Shanedling Hall)

Traditional Communal Style (Lee Hall)

All rooms include the following for each resident:

Bed frame
Highest height: 36-inches from floor

Extra long twin mattress (36″x80″)

Desk and chair

Dresser

Closet space or wardrobe

Carpet

Blinds

Central air conditioning and heat

Trash and recycling bin

Wireless internet

Electricity, gas, and water

View the interactive map

The South 40 includes ten residential communities located at the southwest end of the Danforth Campus that serve as the home to first year students and sophomores. The South 40 is a “small town” with its own fitness center, dining facilities, meeting rooms, basketball and sand volleyball courts, recreation and game rooms, and music practice rooms. In addition, Bear Necessities (gift shop), Paws & Go (convenience store), the Hitzeman Mail Center, printers and copiers, and several 
student-run businesses
 are located nearby. Residence halls located on the South 40 are considered either 
modern
 or 
traditional

First-year housing

All WashU first-year students live on campus. As a first-year, you’ll get to live in one of 10 residential communities on a part of campus we call the “South 40”. Learn more about first-year housing

Sophomore housing

Most sophomores will continue living on the South 40. Some will move to the Northside, while some opt for Affinity Communities such as our Living Learning Communities (LLCs) or Chapter Housing with a Fraternity. Learn more about sophomore housing

Upper division housing

Many of our upper-division (juniors and seniors) students live in our on-campus apartments which are located on the Northwest corner of the Danforth Campus and in our off-campus apartments located in neighborhoods near campus, each of which provides a variety of programs and amenities for students to enjoy. Learn more about upper division housing

Affinity communities 

We are home to both Living Learning Communities (LLC) and Chapter Housing options for students. Learn more about affinity communities

Gender-inclusive housing

Residential Life makes housing assignments by matching students who share the same assigned sex at birth and by reviewing roommate preference questions provided in the housing application. Learn more about gender-inclusivity

Academic break housing. WashU Residential Life provides students the option to stay in their residence halls and apartments during academic breaks. Learn more about academic break housing

Non-Residential Life housing options

Residential Life and WashU are committed to supporting our students, non-students, and the permanent residents that live in the surrounding neighborhoods. Learn more about living off-campus

Summer housing

WashU has many summer housing options for interns, researchers, and summer school students. Conference Services

Your WashU campus neighborhood. All WashU first- and second-year students live on campus. As a first-year, you’ll live in one of 10 residential communities on a part of campus we call the “South 40.”

The South 40

The South 40 is like its own small town, complete with a student government, dining facilities, a fitness center, a technology center, meeting rooms, intramural fields, basketball and sand volleyball courts, and more. A gift shop, a convenience store, a mailroom, and several student-run businesses are located there, too. Explore South 40 residential communities

Community living

As a member of a residential community, you’ll get to know others and become involved in numerous activities and organizations. You’ll be encouraged and supported in your intellectual development and your relationships with others, including students, faculty, and staff. Learn about community living

Residential community resources

Our residential communities have social lounges, study rooms, personal computing labs, and recreation rooms. Each residential community also has faculty, student, and staff members dedicated to helping you pursue your personal and educational goals. Resident Advisors (RAs) are highly trained students who live in the residential communities, who you can count on as leaders, confidants, resources, and friends. Each community also has:

A Residential Community Director (RCD), a professional staff member who works with you and your RAs to provide support and manage day-to-day operations. Its own dedicated housekeeping and maintenance personnel. Learn about residential communities

Student leadership

Through the Congress of the South 40 (CS40), you’ll have the opportunity to participate or even lead community programming within your residential community. You’ll elect representatives who work as part of individual residential community councils to address your needs and plan community-building programs throughout the school year. CS40 sponsors and produces many events on the South 40 – including large sporting events, outdoor music festivals, a formal dance, outings to St. Louis attractions, community service activities, and more – throughout the year. Get involved with CS40

Tour the residential halls from your phone. Our students created video tours of the residence halls. The full playlist is available on our YouTube channel. Watch full playlist

Residential Options

Residence houses located on the South 40 are either modern or traditional buildings. Modern buildings consist primarily of double rooms, with some triple and single rooms. Most room layouts consist of two double rooms adjoined by a restroom. Traditional buildings have single, double, and triple room options on each floor. Each floor community shares common restrooms. Some traditional halls are configured in a suite style with double and single rooms, a living area, and a shared restroom. Apply for first-year on-campus housing

What’s in a Room? Your room comes equipped with an extra-long twin bed, a desk, a chair, a dresser, bookshelves, and a closet.

le rooms adjoined by a restroom. Traditional buildings have single, double, and triple room options on each floor. Each floor community shares common restrooms. Some traditional halls are configured in a suite style with double and single rooms, a living area, and a shared restroom. Apply for first-year on-campus housing

What’s in a Room? Your room comes equipped with an extra-long twin bed, a desk, a chair, a dresser, bookshelves, and a closet. All rooms also have mini-blinds and are air-conditioned. Over the summer, you’ll receive a guide that will include average room dimensions and a list of items to bring to campus. Residential Life virtual housing tours

WashU offers housing options and resources to find off-campus housing for students, scholars and employees. St. Louis, and the surrounding areas, is made up of many neighborhoods with a variety of housing options. On-campus undergraduate student housing

WashU requires all full-time, first-year, degree-seeking undergraduate students to live in the residence halls for the entire academic year. This requirement does not apply to incoming transfer or incoming exchange students. The 
Office of Residential Life
 manages on-campus housing for undergraduate students. On-campus housing for 1st year undergraduate students

All first year undergraduate students live on campus in one of the residential communities on a part of campus referred to as the 
South 40
. The area is nicknamed “The South 40” because it is forty acres located directly south of the Danforth Campus. The space is home to about 3,200 residents in 24 residence halls, as well as recreational space, computer clusters, campus eateries, and much, much more. “The 40” is a place to study, sleep, eat, relax, and form new friendships. Learn more about the South 40 Residential Communities

Room Type Options:
All housing for first-year students is in 
either traditional or modern residential communities
:

Modern buildings
 typically feature suite-style layouts with shared bathrooms among a small group of students. Traditional suite style buildings
 have suite-style layouts with bathrooms among a small group of students. Traditional communal style buildings 
have bathrooms that are maintained by custodial staff and shared by a floor or hallway. The primary difference between Modern & Traditional Suite Style buildings is the presence of an elevator. Modern buildings have elevators while traditional buildings do not. There is no “better” option—it often comes down to personal preference. Both types of communities offer excellent opportunities to meet other students and engage in the first-year experience. A note about single rooms:

Single rooms are 
extremely limited
 for incoming first-year students and are typically reserved for those with approved accommodations through 
Disability Resources
. Most first-year students are assigned to 
double rooms
, where they will share a space with one roommate. On-campus housing for sophomores (2nd year undergraduate students

Most sophomores live on the South 40. Some live on the Northside in the Village. Some will opt for our Special Interest Housing. Learn more about sophomore housing

On-campus housing for upper-division ( 3rd & 4th year) undergraduate and graduate students

Our upper-division students have a wide variety of housing options from which to choose. The area known as The Village sits on the Danforth Campus and includes the suite-style housing of Village House and Lopata House. The Village East and Millbrook Apartments are also on campus. Finally, many of our upper-division students are housed in apartment communities that are located in the neighborhoods surrounding the university.

and graduate students

Our upper-division students have a wide variety of housing options from which to choose. The area known as The Village sits on the Danforth Campus and includes the suite-style housing of Village House and Lopata House. The Village East and Millbrook Apartments are also on campus. Finally, many of our upper-division students are housed in apartment communities that are located in the neighborhoods surrounding the university. Each of these varied communities provide extensive programs and amenities for students to enjoy. Learn more about upper division on campus housing

Graduate-only housing

New graduate housing is now available at Rosedale Court Apartments with various unit sizes, private bathrooms, and fully furnished living spaces. For more information and to express your interest, 
fill out the interest form
. Summer housing

WashU Conference Services has housing options available in the summer for international students who are currently living on campus and wish to stay for the full summer or a portion of the summer, on a fee basis. Reach out to Conference Services at 
conferenceservices
@rourke or visit their 
website
 for more information. Off-campus housing

Most graduate and professional students, scholars and employees live off-campus. Although there are a number of different ways in which you can rent off-campus housing, we have compiled a list of commonly used resources that many of our international students, scholars and employees use. WashU-managed apartments

WashU’s 
Quadrangle
 manages off-campus housing options located mainly in the Skinker-DeBaliviere area, north of the Delmar Loop and in the Central West End area. Many are 10-20 minutes walking distance and accessible by the free WashU shuttle. Students who don’t yet have a WashU key should enter “123456” as their ID and must provide proof of admission. Reach out to 
quadrangle@wustl.edu
 with questions. Parallel Properties
 is another WashU-owned company, which offers similar housing options and services as Quadrangle. Reach out to 
info@rentparallel.com
 with questions. View Parallel properties

Apartment referral services

Apartment Referral Services
 (ARS) is available to all WashU students, faculty and staff to assist with off-campus housing options. On this website, you will find a comprehensive listing of both WashU-owned and non WashU-owned housing options, resources for finding roommates, subleasing information and many additional resources that will help you find the right apartment for you. Admitted students who do not have an active WashU email yet can set up a temporary guest account with ARS. Reach out to 
ars@wustl.edu
 with questions. Temporary Housing

The Core Apartment Residences
 offer a limited number of temporary housing options for WashU guests who wish to stay temporarily. Depending on availability, this is a good option for visiting students, faculty or staff members and individuals pursuing research, internships and educational opportunities.

not have an active WashU email yet can set up a temporary guest account with ARS. Reach out to 
ars@wustl.edu
 with questions. Temporary Housing

The Core Apartment Residences
 offer a limited number of temporary housing options for WashU guests who wish to stay temporarily. Depending on availability, this is a good option for visiting students, faculty or staff members and individuals pursuing research, internships and educational opportunities. Each guest must be a visiting student or otherwise sponsored by a WashU department to be eligible. For more information email 
thecore@wustl.edu
. We recommend the services listed above, but you are not limited by these options. You are free to choose apartment or housing options through non-WashU affiliated listing agents and rental property services. Renting tips

If you decide to rent an apartment or other housing off campus, we strongly recommend that you purchase renter’s insurance. Renter’s insurance covers your personal property in case of fire, damage or theft. You can find an insurance agent by searching online. When renting an apartment and traveling off campus, we recommend that you consider important safety tips. The 
Washington University Police Department (WUPD)
 has a number of resources for safety topics you may be thinking about as you make housing decisions. A list of these resources, including 
Safety Tips
 when off campus and 
Personal Safety
 tips can be found on the WUPD website. Rental housing scams

Many international students and scholars lease and sublet apartments in the St. Louis area. It is important for those who are seeking to rent (or sublease) an apartment to beware of possible scams. According to the 
U.S. Federal Trade Commission
, there are three (3) primary ways to know whether or not an apartment rental opportunity or inquiry is a scam:

They want you to wire money. This is the surest sign of a scam. There’s never a good reason to wire money to pay a security deposit, application fee or first month’s rent. Wiring money is the same as sending cash – once you send it, you have no way to get it back. They want a security deposit or first month’s rent before you’ve met or signed a lease. It’s never a good idea to send money to someone you’ve never met in person for an apartment you haven’t seen. If you can’t visit an apartment or house yourself, ask someone you trust to go and confirm that it’s for rent. In addition to setting up a meeting, do a search on the landlord and listing. If you find the same ad listed under a different name, that’s a clue that it may be a scam. They say they are located outside of the country but have a plan to get the keys into your hands. It might involve a lawyer or “agent” working on their behalf. Some scammers even create fake keys. Be skeptical and don’t send money overseas. If you can’t meet in person, see the apartment, or sign a lease before you pay, keep looking. Explore St. Louis

The St. Louis region is one of the Midwest’s key cultural destinations, boasting an abundance of museums, music and theater venues, sporting events, fine dining and shopping districts.

The St. Louis region is one of the Midwest’s key cultural destinations, boasting an abundance of museums, music and theater venues, sporting events, fine dining and shopping districts. The St. Louis Visitor’s Commission offers 
Explore St. Louis
, a website including St. Louis dining, events, cultural activities, children’s activities and transportation. It is an authority on what to see and do in the St. Louis area. For more information about St. Louis and the neighborhoods surrounding WashU’s campuses, explore 
living in St. Louis
 on the central WashU website.
